segments.inferno-10-g1: “Ora” means “now,” opening the narrative in the present. “Sen va” is an archaic contraction of “se ne va,” literally “goes away/goes along,” and “per” here means motion along or through a route; “un secreto calle” is “a hidden/secluded path.”
segments.inferno-10-g2: This prepositional phrase specifies where the path runs: “tra” means “between.” “’l” is a contraction of “il,” and “de la” is archaic/poetic for modern “della”; “terra” here means the city, while “li martìri” means the punishments or torments of the damned.
segments.inferno-10-g3: “Lo mio maestro” is the delayed subject of “sen va”: Dante places it after the verb and intervening prepositional phrases for poetic order. “Lo” before “mio” is an archaic form of the masculine article, equivalent to modern “il mio maestro.”
segments.inferno-10-g4: This elliptical clause omits the verb already understood from “sen va”: “and I [go] behind his back.” “Dopo le spalle” literally means “after the shoulders,” an idiom for following behind someone.
segments.inferno-10-g5: This is a vocative address to Virgil: “O” introduces direct address, and “virtù somma” means “supreme power” or “highest excellence.” The adjective “somma” follows the noun, a normal but elevated Italian order.
segments.inferno-10-g6: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “virtù somma”: “who/which.” “Per li empi giri” means “through the wicked circles,” and “mi volvi” is a second-person singular present form meaning “you turn/guide me,” with “mi” as the direct object.
segments.inferno-10-g7: “Cominciai” is first-person singular passato remoto: “I began.” In context it means “I began to speak,” and it interrupts the quoted speech as a reporting clause.
segments.inferno-10-g8: “Com’” is the elided form of “come,” here meaning “as.” “A te piace” is an impersonal construction: literally “it pleases to you,” meaning “as you please” or “as seems good to you.”
segments.inferno-10-g9: “Parlami” is the imperative “parla” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi”: “speak to me.” The singular imperative matches Dante’s address to Virgil.
segments.inferno-10-g10: “Sodisfammi” is another imperative with enclitic “mi”: “satisfy me” or “answer me fully.” “A’” is a contraction for “a i/ai,” and “miei disiri” is an archaic form of “miei desideri,” “my desires.”
segments.inferno-10-g11: “La gente” is the subject, modified by the relative clause “che per li sepolcri giace,” meaning “who lie among/throughout the tombs.” “Potrebbesi” is an archaic enclitic form of “si potrebbe,” an impersonal/passive construction: literally “could one see,” naturally “could they be seen?”; “veder” is the apocopated form of “vedere.”
segments.inferno-10-g12: “Già” means “already,” and “son” is the shortened form of “sono.” “Levati” is a past participle agreeing with the plural subject “tutt’ i coperchi,” so the phrase means “all the lids are raised/lifted.”
segments.inferno-10-g13: “Nessun” means “no one” here, functioning as the subject. “Guardia face” is an archaic/poetic form of “fa guardia,” literally “does guard,” meaning “keeps watch”; “face” is therefore a verb form, not the noun meaning “torch.”
segments.inferno-10-g24: The speaker addresses Dante with the vocative “O Tosco,” and the relative “che” introduces a clause describing him. “Per la città del foco” means motion through the fiery city, while “vivo” is emphatic because Dante is alive among the dead; “ten vai” is poetic for “te ne vai,” ‘you go along/away,’ and “parlando onesto” uses the gerund to describe the manner of his going, with “onesto” functioning adverbially as ‘honorably/courteously.’
segments.inferno-10-g25: “Piacciati” is an archaic courteous form of “piaccia a te,” literally ‘may it please you,’ here equivalent to ‘please/deign.’ It governs the infinitive with “di,” so “di restare” means ‘to remain,’ and “in questo loco” gives the place where Dante is asked to stay.
segments.inferno-10-g26: “La tua loquela” is the subject: Dante’s way of speaking, his language or accent. “Ti fa manifesto” means ‘makes you manifest/reveals you,’ and “di quella nobil patrïa natio” identifies what he is revealed as: a native of that noble homeland, with “natio” agreeing with “ti” understood as Dante.
segments.inferno-10-g27: “A la qual” is the medieval spelling of modern “alla quale,” ‘to which,’ referring back to the “nobil patrïa,” Florence. “Fui” is the passato remoto of “essere,” and “troppo molesto” means ‘too troublesome/burdensome,’ with “forse” softening the admission as ‘perhaps.’
segments.inferno-10-g28: “Subitamente” sets the sudden timing, and “questo suono” is the subject of “uscìo,” an archaic form of “uscì,” ‘issued/came out.’ The phrase “d’una de l’arche” means ‘from one of the tombs,’ with “de l’” representing “delle” in older spelling.
segments.inferno-10-g29: “Però” here has its older logical sense ‘therefore,’ not the modern common sense ‘however.’ “M’accostai” is “mi accostai,” a reflexive passato remoto meaning ‘I drew near’ or ‘I moved closer.’
segments.inferno-10-g30: “Temendo” is a gerund from “temere,” giving the circumstance or motive for the action: Dante moved closer while fearing or because he was afraid. It modifies the subject of “m’accostai.”
segments.inferno-10-g31: This segment completes “m’accostai”: Dante drew “un poco più,” ‘a little more,’ toward Virgil. “Al duca mio” means ‘to my guide,’ with “duca” used in its guiding sense rather than as a noble title.
segments.inferno-10-g32: “Ed” simply links the action to what precedes. “El” is an archaic or poetic form for “egli,” ‘he,’ referring to Dante’s guide, and “mi disse” uses “mi” as an indirect object: ‘said to me.’
segments.inferno-10-g33: “Volgiti” is the imperative of “volgere” with the reflexive pronoun “ti” attached. It commands Dante to turn himself around or turn toward the speaker.
segments.inferno-10-g82: This is a negative question: non frames the whole clause, while viv’ is vive with final -e elided before elli. Elli is an archaic/poetic form of egli, “he,” and ancora means “still” here.
segments.inferno-10-g83: The verb fiere is not the adjective “fierce” here, but an archaic form of ferire, “to strike”; the subject comes after the object: lo dolce lume “the sweet light” strikes li occhi suoi “his eyes.” Li is the old masculine plural article for modern gli/i, and lo before dolce is the old masculine singular article.
segments.inferno-10-g84: This segment contains only punctuation, closing the preceding quoted question.
segments.inferno-10-g85: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause, “when.” S’accorse is reflexive in form, from accorgersi, meaning “became aware/noticed,” and d’alcuna dimora supplies what he noticed; ch’io facëa is a relative clause, with facëa an archaic imperfect form of facea/facevo, “I was making,” referring to the delay before la risposta, “the answer.”
segments.inferno-10-g86: Supin is a truncated poetic form of supino, “supine, on his back,” used adverbially with ricadde, “he fell back.” E più non parve fora means “and he no longer appeared outside”; più non is the negative expression “no longer,” while fora is an old form meaning “outside/out,” not a form of essere here.
segments.inferno-10-g61: Allor means “then/thereupon,” marking a new action after the previous exchange. Surse is an archaic preterite form of sorgere, “rose up”; the subject comes after the verb, un’ombra, a poetic inversion. A la vista scoperchiata literally suggests “at the uncovered sight/opening,” referring to the open tomb from which the shade becomes visible.
segments.inferno-10-g62: Lungo is used adverbially/prepositionally as “along” or “beside.” Questa is feminine singular and points back to the nearby tomb or sepulchral place already in view: the shade rises alongside “this one.”
segments.inferno-10-g63: Infino is an archaic/poetic form meaning “as far as” or “up to.” Al is the contraction of a + il, and the phrase gives the visible extent of the shade’s emergence: up to the chin.
segments.inferno-10-g64: Credo introduces Dante’s inference: “I think/believe that...” S’era levata is reflexive plus pluperfect with essere, “had raised itself / had risen”; in ginocchie is an idiom meaning “on its knees,” with the plural form ginocchie.
segments.inferno-10-g65: Dintorno modifies guardò and means that he looked around or round about Dante. Mi is the object of this looking-around, best understood as “around me,” and guardò is a third-person singular past tense: “he looked.”
segments.inferno-10-g66: Come here means “as if,” introducing a comparison with an implied unreal or uncertain state; accordingly avesse is imperfect subjunctive. Talento in medieval Italian often means “desire,” so talento avesse di veder means “he had a desire to see.” S’altri is an elision of se altri, “whether someone else,” not reflexive si; meco is the archaic compound form “with me.”
segments.inferno-10-g67: E poi che means “and after/when,” with che completing the temporal conjunction. ’l is the elided article il before sospecciar, a nominalized infinitive meaning “the suspecting” or “suspicion.” Fu spento is a passive-like perfect construction, “was extinguished,” and tutto functions adverbially as “completely.”
segments.inferno-10-g68: Piangendo is a gerund meaning “weeping” and describes the manner in which he speaks. Disse is the preterite of dire, “he said,” introducing the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-10-g69: Se is the conditional conjunction “if,” though the provided sense list does not include that value. Per questo cieco carcere means “through this blind/dark prison,” with per expressing movement through a place. The second per means “by means of,” so per altezza d’ingegno means that Dante travels by virtue of the loftiness or greatness of his intellect.
segments.inferno-10-g70: Mio figlio is placed before the interrogative for emotional emphasis: “my son—where is he?” Ov’ is the elided form of ove, “where,” before è. È here means “is located / is,” asking where the son is.
segments.inferno-10-g71: E links this question to the preceding one inside the same direct speech. Perché introduces a direct question, “why,” and non negates è. Teco is an archaic compound form meaning “with you,” parallel to meco in the previous sentence.
segments.inferno-10-g72: This segment contains only punctuation and has no grammatical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-10-g14: This is an elliptical speech tag: the verb of saying is omitted, so it means “and that one to me,” i.e. “and he said to me.” “Quelli” is a pronoun referring to the previously speaking person, and “a me” marks the indirect object.
segments.inferno-10-g15: “Saran” is a poetic shortened future form of “saranno,” and with “serrati” forms a future passive/adjectival phrase: “will be shut.” “Quando ... torneranno” gives the time clause; “di Iosafàt” means “from Jehoshaphat,” the valley associated with the Last Judgment, and “coi corpi che ... hanno lasciati” adds the bodies left “up there” in the living world.
segments.inferno-10-g16: The object “Suo cimitero” is placed first for poetic emphasis, while the subject comes later: “tutti suoi seguaci, con Epicuro.” “Da questa parte” means “on this side / in this section,” and “hanno” is the present-tense verb governing the whole clause.
segments.inferno-10-g17: “Che” introduces a relative clause describing Epicurus and his followers. The phrase “l’anima col corpo morta fanno” has a poetic word order: object “l’anima,” accompaniment “col corpo,” predicate adjective “morta,” then verb “fanno,” literally “make the soul dead with the body.”
segments.inferno-10-g18: “Però” means “therefore,” drawing a conclusion from what has just been said. “A la dimanda ... satisfatto sarà” is an impersonal/passive-like construction: literally, “to the question ... satisfaction will be made,” meaning it will be answered; “faci” is an archaic second-person form of “fai,” here “put/ask,” and “quinc’ entro” means “in here.”
segments.inferno-10-g19: This segment is coordinated with the previous object: the promised satisfaction applies not only to the stated question but also “al disio,” to the desire. “Che tu mi taci” is a relative clause meaning “which you keep silent from me / do not mention to me,” with “mi” marking the person from whom it is withheld.
segments.inferno-10-g20: This is another brief speech tag. “E io” is elliptical for “and I said,” with the finite verb understood from the dialogue structure.
segments.inferno-10-g21: “Buon duca” is a direct address to the guide. “Duca” here has its older sense of “leader” or “guide,” not the noble title “duke.”
segments.inferno-10-g22: The word order is poetic: “mio cuor” is the object of “tegno riposto,” so the sense is “I do not keep my heart hidden from you.” “Se non” functions as “except,” and “per dicer poco” uses the apocopated infinitive “dicer” for “dire,” meaning “in order to speak little.”
segments.inferno-10-g23: “M’hai ... disposto” is a compound tense with “hai” plus past participle, with “m’” as the direct object: “you have disposed/prepared me.” “Non pur mo” means “not only now,” implying that the guide had already instructed him before to be brief; “a ciò” means “for this / toward this purpose.”
segments.inferno-10-g34: Che is the direct interrogative “what,” placed first as usual in a question. Fai is the 2nd-person singular present indicative of fare, addressed directly to Dante: “what are you doing?”
segments.inferno-10-g35: Vedi is a 2nd-person singular imperative-like form, “see/behold,” introducing what Dante is to look at. Che begins a relative clause referring to Farinata; s’è is si è, a reflexive/pronominal construction, and dritto means “upright,” so the phrase means that he has raised himself or is standing upright.
segments.inferno-10-g36: Da la is the older separated form of modern dalla, “from the,” and da la cintola in sù means “from the waist upward.” Tutto ’l vedrai has poetic word order: tutto means “all/whole,” ’l is an elided object pronoun lo, “him,” and vedrai is future, “you will see.”
segments.inferno-10-g37: Avea is the archaic/poetic form of aveva, the imperfect of avere. The construction avere + object + adjective/participle gives “I had my gaze fixed”; nel suo is elliptical, meaning “in/on his [gaze/face].”
segments.inferno-10-g38: El is an old form for egli, “he,” and s’ergea is si ergeva, imperfect, “was rising/held himself up.” Com’ avesse is short for come se avesse, with the imperfect subjunctive in a comparison contrary to fact or imagined; avere l’inferno a gran dispitto is an idiom meaning “to hold Hell in great scorn.”
segments.inferno-10-g39: L’animose man del duca e pronte is a poetic noun phrase: l’ stands for le, man is an abbreviated plural form for mani, and the adjectives animose and pronte surround the noun. Pinser is a contracted old passato remoto plural form meaning “pushed,” with mi as its direct object; a lui means “toward him.”
segments.inferno-10-g40: Dicendo is the gerund of dire, “saying,” and gives an accompanying action. Logically it refers to the guide as he urges Dante forward, even though the immediately preceding grammatical subject is “the hands.”
segments.inferno-10-g41: Le parole tue has the possessive after the noun, a normal poetic and emphatic order: “your words.” Sien is an archaic subjunctive form of siano used jussively, “let them be”; conte is an old adjective meaning “known, clear, well weighed/considered,” not the noun “count.”
segments.inferno-10-g117: “El par” is an impersonal construction, literally “it seems”; “El” is an archaic/poetic form for “it.” The clause “che voi veggiate” depends on “par,” with “veggiate” used as a subjunctive form of “vedere” in the sense “you see,” though the supplied lemma points to a different verb.
segments.inferno-10-g118: “Se ben odo” is a parenthetical condition meaning “if I hear rightly.” “Odo” is first-person singular present, and “ben” functions adverbially, modifying the hearing.
segments.inferno-10-g119: “Dinanzi” here means “beforehand,” referring to foreknowledge of future events. “Quel che” means “that which/what,” and “’l tempo seco adduce” has poetic word order: “time brings with itself,” with “seco” meaning “with it.”
segments.inferno-10-g120: This clause contrasts future knowledge with knowledge of the present: “and in the present you keep/have another manner.” “Tenete altro modo” means “you have a different way/mode,” with “modo” referring to the manner in which they know things.
segments.inferno-10-g121: “Noi veggiam” is the subject and verb: “we see.” “Veggiam” is a shortened poetic form of “veggiamo,” first-person plural present of “vedere.”
segments.inferno-10-g122: “Come” introduces a comparison: “like one who has poor sight.” “Quei” means “that one/he who,” and “c’ha” is a contraction of “che ha”; “mala luce” idiomatically means weak or defective vision, though “mala” is not represented by the supplied senses.
segments.inferno-10-g123: “Le cose” is the direct object of “veggiam”: “the things.” It is then specified by the following relative clause, “che ne son lontano,” meaning the things that are far away from them.
segments.inferno-10-g124: “Disse” is a narrative insertion, “he said,” interrupting the quoted speech. It is the third-person singular past historic of “dire.”
segments.inferno-10-g125: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “le cose.” “Ne son lontano” means “are distant from us”; “son” is plural here, while “lontano” is an invariable/adverbial-looking form used where modern Italian would expect agreement.
segments.inferno-10-g126: “Cotanto” means “so much/to that extent,” explaining the limited degree of their vision. “Il sommo duce” is God, “the supreme Leader,” and “ne splende” means that his light still shines on or for them; the pronoun “ne” has a dative sense not covered well by the listed senses.
segments.inferno-10-g127: “Quando” introduces a temporal clause referring to the things/events just mentioned. “S’appressano” is reflexive/pronominal, “draw near,” and “o son” means “or are,” with “present” understood from the context.
segments.inferno-10-g128: The main clause says that their understanding fails when events become near or present. “Tutto” is adverbial in force, “wholly/entirely,” while “vano” means “vain, useless,” a sense not included in the supplied list.
segments.inferno-10-g129: “E s’altri non ci apporta” means “and if another does not bring it to us.” Here “s’” is a contraction of “se,” “if,” not a reflexive pronoun; “altri” has the old indefinite sense “someone else/another person,” and “ci” is indirect object, “to us.”
segments.inferno-10-g130: “Nulla sapem” is poetic for “we know nothing,” with “sapem” a shortened form of “sappiamo.” “Di vostro stato umano” means “about/of your human condition,” referring to the condition of the living.
segments.inferno-10-g131: “Però” here means “therefore,” drawing a conclusion from the preceding explanation. “Comprender puoi” uses poetic inversion for “puoi comprendere,” and the following “che” introduces the object clause; “fia” is an archaic future form meaning “will be.” “Da quel punto che” means “from that moment when,” and “del futuro fia chiusa la porta” is a passive construction: “the gate of the future will be closed.”
segments.inferno-10-g42: “Com’” is an elided form of “come” used temporally here, meaning “when/as soon as.” “Al piè de la sua tomba fui” has poetic word order: the locative phrase comes before “fui,” the first-person passato remoto of “essere,” meaning “I was/came to be at the foot of his tomb.”
segments.inferno-10-g43: “Guardommi” is an archaic enclitic form: “guardò mi,” literally “he looked at me.” “Un poco” functions adverbially, meaning “for a little while” or “a little.”
segments.inferno-10-g44: “E poi” coordinates the next action with the previous one: after looking at Dante, the speaker proceeds to ask a question. “Poi” marks narrative sequence, “then/afterwards.”
segments.inferno-10-g45: “Quasi sdegnoso” is a parenthetical manner phrase describing how he speaks. “Quasi” means “as if,” and “sdegnoso” is a masculine singular adjective agreeing with the implied subject, the shade in the tomb.
segments.inferno-10-g46: “Mi dimandò” means “he asked me”; “dimandò” is passato remoto, the normal narrative past tense in Dante. The object pronoun “mi” precedes the verb, as in standard Italian.
segments.inferno-10-g47: The direct question uses “Chi” as “who.” “Fuor” is an archaic form for “furono,” “were,” and “li maggior tui” literally means “your greater/older ones,” idiomatically “your ancestors” or “forebears.”
segments.inferno-10-g48: This segment contains only the full stop and has no lexical or grammatical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-10-g49: “Io ch’era” means “I, who was,” with “ch’” elided before the vowel of “era.” “D’ubidir disideroso” places the complement before the adjective in a poetic construction: “desirous/eager of obeying,” i.e. “eager to obey.”
segments.inferno-10-g50: “Non gliel celai” means “I did not conceal it from him.” “Gliel” combines “gli” plus “lo,” “to him it,” and “celai” is first-person passato remoto of “celare.”
segments.inferno-10-g51: “Ma” contrasts with the preceding negative: not hiding it, but revealing it. “Tutto gliel’ apersi” literally says “I opened it all to him,” where “apersi” is used figuratively for “disclosed/revealed.”
segments.inferno-10-g52: “Ond’” means “whereupon/therefore,” linking his reaction to Dante’s answer. “Ei levò le ciglia un poco in suso” has the subject “ei” before the verb; “in suso” is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning “upward.”
segments.inferno-10-g53: “Poi disse” resumes the sequence of actions after the gesture: “then he said.” “Disse” is passato remoto of “dire,” used for the historical narrative past.
segments.inferno-10-g54: “Fieramente furo avversi” means “they were fiercely hostile,” with “furo” an archaic shortened form of “furono.” The repeated “a” introduces the targets of hostility: “to me,” “to my ancestors/forebears,” and “to my party/side.”
segments.inferno-10-g55: “Sì che” introduces a result clause: “so that.” “Per due fïate” means “on two occasions/twice,” and “li dispersi” means “I scattered them,” with “li” as an object pronoun referring to the opposing group.
segments.inferno-10-g56: “S’ei” is an elision of “se ei,” meaning “if they,” not a reflexive construction here. “Fur cacciati” is a passive passato remoto form: “they were driven out.”
segments.inferno-10-g57: “Ei tornar” means “they returned,” with “tornar” an archaic third-person plural passato remoto form. “D’ogne parte” means “from every side/quarter,” expressing return from all directions.
segments.inferno-10-g58: “Rispuos’ io lui” has poetic inversion: the verb comes before the subject, “replied I.” “Lui” functions as the object of address, effectively “to him,” though formally it is the pronoun “him.”
segments.inferno-10-g59: “L’una e l’altra fïata” means “the one and the other time,” idiomatically “both times.” The phrase refers back to the two occasions just mentioned.
segments.inferno-10-g60: “Ma” introduces Dante’s sharp contrast: his side returned, but Farinata’s side did not. “I vostri” means “your people,” and “appreser” is an archaic form of “appresero,” here meaning “learned”; “quell’ arte” is “that skill/art,” the skill of returning from exile.
segments.inferno-10-g162: “Appresso” means “afterward/then,” linking this action to what has just been said. “Mosse” is the simple past, third-person singular of “muovere,” here with “il piede” as its object; “a man sinistra” is an idiom meaning “to/on the left-hand side.”
segments.inferno-10-g163: “Lasciammo” and “gimmo” are first-person plural simple past forms: “we left” and, archaically, “we went.” “Inver’ lo mezzo” means “toward the middle,” with elision in “inver’,” and “per un sentier” means “along/by way of a path.” The relative “ch’” introduces a clause describing the path; “fiede” literally means “strikes/hits,” used poetically for a path that reaches or runs into a valley.
segments.inferno-10-g164: The “che” is relative, referring back to “valle”: the valley is the source of the stench. “’nfin” is a contraction of “infino,” meaning “as far as/even to,” and “là sù” means “up there.” “Facea” is an archaic imperfect of “fare” in a causative construction with the infinitive “spiacer”: literally, “made its stench displease,” i.e. “made its stench offensive.”
segments.inferno-10-g73: This is an elliptical speech tag: the verb of saying is omitted, as often in Dante, so it means “And I said to him.” “A lui” is an indirect-object phrase, “to him.”
segments.inferno-10-g74: “Da me stesso” means “from/by myself,” idiomatically “on my own initiative” or “of my own accord,” not merely “alone.” “Vegno” is an archaic/poetic first-person present form of “venire,” modern Italian “vengo.”
segments.inferno-10-g75: “Colui ch’attende là” is the subject of the following verb “mena”: “the one who waits there.” “Ch’” is elided before a vowel and functions as the relative pronoun “who.”
segments.inferno-10-g76: “Per qui” is placed before the verb and means “through here/through this place”; “mi mena” has the object pronoun before the verb, “leads me.” The final relative clause “cui Guido vostro ebbe a disdegno” uses “cui” for “whom” with the idiom “avere a disdegno,” “to hold in disdain”; “ebbe” is passato remoto, and “forse” qualifies the claim as uncertain.
segments.inferno-10-g77: The compound subject is “Le sue parole e ’l modo de la pena,” so the auxiliary is plural: “m’avean ... letto.” “Avean” is an apocopated imperfect form of “avevano,” and “m’” is indirect, “to me”; “di costui” depends on “il nome,” meaning “the name of this man.”
segments.inferno-10-g78: “Però” has its older causal sense here, “therefore,” linking this clause to the previous explanation. “Fu” is passato remoto of “essere,” and “così piena” means that Dante’s answer was “so full/complete.”
segments.inferno-10-g79: “Di sùbito” is an adverbial phrase meaning “suddenly” or “all at once.” “Drizzato” is a past participle used with the understood subject, “he,” describing him as raised or straightened upright before the main verb “gridò,” a passato remoto form meaning “cried out.”
segments.inferno-10-g80: “Come?” is an abrupt interrogative adverb, “How?” It introduces the speaker’s startled reaction to Dante’s past-tense wording.
segments.inferno-10-g81: “Dicesti” is second-person singular passato remoto: “did you say?” The quoted phrase “elli ebbe” repeats Dante’s wording; “elli” is an archaic subject pronoun meaning “he,” and “ebbe” is the past tense “had,” which prompts the speaker’s alarm.
segments.inferno-10-g149: “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” the subject pronoun “he,” referring to Virgil. “Si mosse” is a reflexive/pronominal passato remoto from “muovere,” meaning “he moved/set off”; the listed lemma and senses for “mosse” do not match this use.
segments.inferno-10-g150: “E poi” links this action to the previous one with simple coordination: “and then.” “Poi” marks the next moment in the narrative sequence.
segments.inferno-10-g151: “Così andando” is a gerund phrase, literally “thus going,” giving the circumstance under which he speaks. The gerund “andando” is simultaneous with “disse”: while they were proceeding in this way.
segments.inferno-10-g152: “Mi” is an indirect object pronoun, “to me,” placed before the verb. “Disse” is passato remoto of “dire,” the normal narrative past tense in Dante: “he said.”
segments.inferno-10-g153: “Perché” introduces a direct question, “why.” “Se’” is the contracted second-person singular of “sei,” and the explicit “tu” adds emphasis; “sì” means “so,” modifying “smarrito,” a past participle used adjectivally: “bewildered, troubled.”
segments.inferno-10-g154: This segment contains only punctuation, marking a sentence division in the supplied text. It has no lexical or grammatical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-10-g155: “E io” resumes the narrative with the explicit subject “I.” “Li” is an archaic dative pronoun meaning “to him,” though that sense is not in the supplied list; “sodisfeci” is passato remoto and here means “I answered/satisfied,” while “al suo dimando” means “to his question” or “as regards his question.”
segments.inferno-10-g156: “La mente tua” is the subject, with the possessive placed after the noun in a poetic but normal Italian order: “your mind.” “Conservi” is a present subjunctive used jussively, “let it preserve/keep”; “quel ch’udito hai” means “that which you have heard,” with “ch’” for “che” and the participle “udito” placed before the auxiliary “hai” in poetic word order. “Contra te” means “against you,” referring to the ominous words Dante has just heard.
segments.inferno-10-g157: The reporting clause follows the quoted command. “Mi” is dative, “to me,” “comandò” is passato remoto, “commanded/enjoined,” and “quel saggio” means “that sage,” referring to Virgil.
segments.inferno-10-g158: This is another direct command inside quotation marks. “Ora” means “now,” and “attendi” is a second-person singular imperative of “attendere,” here meaning “pay heed” or “pay attention,” followed by “qui,” “here.”
segments.inferno-10-g159: The narration resumes with “e,” coordinating Virgil’s gesture with his words. “Drizzò” is passato remoto of “drizzare,” here “raised” or “pointed,” and “’l” is the elided article “il” before “dito,” “finger.”
segments.inferno-10-g160: “Quando sarai” begins a future temporal clause: “when you will be/are.” “Dinanzi al dolce raggio” means “before the sweet radiance,” and “di quella il cui bell’occhio tutto vede” identifies the woman by a relative construction: literally “of that one whose beautiful eye sees everything.”
segments.inferno-10-g161: “Da lei” means “from her,” placed first for emphasis. “Saprai” is the future of “sapere,” “you will know/learn,” and “di tua vita il vïaggio” has poetic word order for “il viaggio di tua vita,” “the journey/course of your life”; the diaeresis in “vïaggio” marks the word’s metrical pronunciation.
segments.inferno-10-g132: “Allor” is a temporal adverb, placing Dante’s reply immediately after what has just been explained. It introduces the main narrative action, “dissi.”
segments.inferno-10-g133: “come” means “as,” introducing a comparison of Dante’s emotional state. “di mia colpa compunto” is a compact poetic phrase: “compunto” is a past participle/adjective meaning contrite or pierced with remorse, and “di mia colpa” gives the cause, “by/for my fault.”
segments.inferno-10-g134: “dissi” is the first-person singular preterite of “dire,” the normal narrative tense for a completed action: “I said.” It governs the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-10-g135: “Or direte dunque” uses the future “direte,” addressed respectfully or plurally to Farinata, with the sense of an instruction: “now then you will tell.” The object of telling is “a quel caduto,” and the content is introduced by “che”; “’l suo nato” literally means “his born one,” i.e. his son, and “co’ vivi” is a contraction of “con i vivi,” “with the living.”
segments.inferno-10-g136: “e s’i’ fui” means “and if I was”; here “s’” is not reflexive but elided “se,” “if,” before “i’,” the poetic form of “io.” “fui” is the preterite of “essere,” referring to Dante’s earlier silence as a completed past fact.
segments.inferno-10-g137: “dianzi” is an adverb meaning “just now” or “a little while ago.” It modifies “fui ... muto,” specifying when Dante was silent.
segments.inferno-10-g138: “a la” is the uncontracted form corresponding to modern “alla.” The phrase “a la risposta muto” means “silent in answer” or “mute with respect to the answer,” completing the conditional clause begun by “s’i’ fui.”
segments.inferno-10-g139: “fate ... saper” is a causative imperative: “make him know,” i.e. “let him know”; the small “i” represents an old dative pronoun, “to him,” not the article. “’l fei” means “I did it,” with “’l” = “lo,” and “perché pensava” gives the reason; “m’avete soluto” means “you have freed/released me,” with “che” standing for the error from which Dante has been released.
segments.inferno-10-g140: “E già” marks that Virgil’s summons is already taking place. “richiamava” is imperfect, suggesting an ongoing action in the background: “my master was calling me back,” with “mi” as direct object.
segments.inferno-10-g141: “per ch’i’” is “per che io,” meaning “wherefore/for which reason I”; the elided “i’” is the subject “I.” “pregai” is preterite, while “che mi dicesse” uses the imperfect subjunctive after a verb of requesting; “più avaccio” means “more quickly,” because Dante is being called away.
segments.inferno-10-g142: “Dissemi” is “disse mi,” with the object pronoun attached after the verb: “he said to me.” This enclitic placement is common in older literary Italian.
segments.inferno-10-g143: “Qui” locates the speaker in the tomb, and “giaccio” is present tense, “I lie.” “più di” forms the comparison “more than,” with “mille” understood as “a thousand others.”
segments.inferno-10-g144: “qua dentro” intensifies the location: “in here.” “è” is singular though two names follow, a poetic or loose agreement with the first named person, “’l secondo Federico”; “’l Cardinale” then adds another figure with “e.”
segments.inferno-10-g145: “de li” is the older separated form of modern “degli,” here meaning “of/about the others.” “mi taccio” is a pronominal expression meaning “I keep silent,” so the phrase means that he leaves the remaining names unmentioned.
segments.inferno-10-g146: “Indi” is a narrative adverb meaning “then.” “s’ascose” is reflexive, from “ascondersi,” “he hid himself” or “withdrew,” describing the spirit’s disappearance.
segments.inferno-10-g147: “inver’” is an apocopated poetic form meaning “toward.” The object “i passi” follows the verb “volsi”: literally “I turned the steps,” a poetic way of saying “I turned my steps,” with “l’antico poeta” referring to Virgil.
segments.inferno-10-g148: “ripensando” is a gerund, expressing an accompanying action: Dante walks while thinking back. “quel parlar” is a substantivized infinitive meaning “that speech/utterance,” and “che mi parea nemico” is a relative clause: “which seemed hostile to me,” with “mi” as an indirect object.
segments.inferno-10-g103: “Ond’” is an elided form of “onde,” used here with the connective sense “therefore/whereupon.” The verb of speaking is omitted poetically: “io a lui” means “I [said/replied] to him.”
segments.inferno-10-g104: The noun phrase “Lo strazio e ’l grande scempio” is the subject of the later verb “fa”; “’l” is the contracted article “il.” The relative clause “che fece l’Arbia colorata in rosso” has “che” referring back to the carnage/slaughter as the thing that “made” the river Arbia “colored red,” with “colorata in rosso” as an object complement.
segments.inferno-10-g105: “Tal orazion” means “such a prayer/speech,” referring back to the hostile sentiment just explained. “Fa far” is a causative construction, literally “makes be made” or “causes to be made,” with “nel nostro tempio” giving the place where these prayers or proposals are uttered.
segments.inferno-10-g106: “Poi ch’” is “poi che,” a temporal conjunction meaning “after/when.” “Ebbe ... mosso” is a past anterior form, “had moved,” and “sospirando” is a gerund describing the accompanying action, “sighing.”
segments.inferno-10-g107: “A ciò” means “in that matter” or “for that deed,” linking back to the proposal being discussed. “Fu’” is the apocopated first-person past form “fui,” so the clause means “I was not alone,” with “sol” as an archaic shortened form of “solo.”
segments.inferno-10-g108: “Disse” is the third-person singular passato remoto of “dire,” introducing the speaker’s direct quotation. Its position after the quoted words is normal narrative inversion: “he said.”
segments.inferno-10-g109: “Né” continues the negative force from the previous clause: “nor.” “Sanza” is the archaic form of modern “senza,” and “sarei mosso” uses the conditional with a past participle, meaning “I would have moved/acted,” understood as acting together with the others.
segments.inferno-10-g110: “Ma” marks a contrast with his denial that he acted alone in the former matter. “Fu’” again stands for “fui,” first-person passato remoto, and “solo” is the predicate adjective: “I was alone.”
segments.inferno-10-g111: “Là dove” introduces the setting, “there where.” “Sofferto fu” is an inverted passive, “was allowed/accepted,” while “per ciascun” gives the agent, “by everyone”; “di tòrre via Fiorenza” is the infinitive clause expressing what was accepted, namely to remove or destroy Florence.
segments.inferno-10-g112: “Colui” resumes the emphatic “io solo”: “I was the one who.” The verb “difesi” is first-person passato remoto, agreeing in sense with “io,” while “la” is a pronoun referring to Florence; “a viso aperto” is an idiom meaning openly or boldly, literally “with open face.”
segments.inferno-10-g113: “Deh” is an archaic interjection used to introduce an earnest plea, roughly “ah, please.” It sets a supplicatory tone for the request that follows.
segments.inferno-10-g114: “Se riposi mai vostra semenza” is an optative or oath-like formula: “may your offspring ever rest.” “Riposi” is present subjunctive, with the singular subject “vostra semenza,” meaning Farinata’s descendants or lineage.
segments.inferno-10-g115: “Prega’” is an apocopated form of “pregai,” first-person passato remoto: “I begged.” The word order “prega’ io lui” is poetic but straightforward: subject “io,” object “lui.”
segments.inferno-10-g116: “Solvetemi” is an imperative with the enclitic pronoun “mi”: “resolve/untie for me.” “Quel nodo” is figurative, a difficulty or crux, and “ha ’nviluppata” is a perfect tense with the apheretic form of “inviluppata,” “entangled,” agreeing in older style with the feminine object “mia sentenza.”
segments.inferno-10-g87: Ma marks a contrast with the preceding action. quell’ altro magnanimo is a noun phrase, literally “that other high-minded one,” where altro distinguishes this figure from the other speaker and magnanimo is used substantively for a noble or great-souled person.
segments.inferno-10-g88: a cui posta is elliptical: posta is a feminine past participle used like a noun, “the question/request put,” and a cui links it back to the magnanimo, “at whose prompting/question.” restato m’era is an inverted pluperfect construction, with the past participle before the auxiliary: “I had remained/stopped”; m’ is an elided mi attached to the verb phrase.
segments.inferno-10-g89: non mutò is a simple past negative, “did not change.” aspetto here means his outward look or expression, so the clause says that his face showed no alteration.
segments.inferno-10-g90: né continues the series of negatives: “nor.” mosse is from muovere, “moved,” though the provided lemma list does not give that sense; collo is the direct object, “neck.”
segments.inferno-10-g91: This is another coordinated negative clause introduced by né. piegò is simple past, “bent,” and sua costa means “his side/flank,” emphasizing that he did not shift his body at all.
segments.inferno-10-g92: e links this participial phrase to the preceding description. sé continüando al primo detto is a poetic reflexive expression meaning that he “connected himself back to” or resumed his earlier speech; al primo detto means “to the first/former words.”
segments.inferno-10-g93: S’ is elided se, “if,” before elli; elli is an archaic form for “they.” han is shortened hanno, “have,” and quell’ arte is the object whose participial complement comes in the next segment.
segments.inferno-10-g94: disse is the simple past of dire, “he said.” It frames the quoted words and refers back to the magnanimo as the speaker.
segments.inferno-10-g95: male appresa completes the verb phrase han ... appresa: “have learned badly.” appresa is feminine singular because it agrees with arte, the object named in the previous segment.
segments.inferno-10-g96: ciò, “that,” refers to the idea that they have learned the art badly. mi tormenta is present tense, “torments me,” and più che forms the comparison “more than”; questo letto is the fiery tomb or bed in which he lies.
segments.inferno-10-g97: Ma introduces a corrective or adversative turn. non cinquanta volte fia raccesa uses fia, an archaic future of essere, with a passive participle: “will be rekindled”; the phrase refers poetically to the repeated lighting of the face of the ruling lady. la donna che qui regge means “the lady who rules here,” with che as the relative pronoun and regge in the present tense.
segments.inferno-10-g98: che after a negative expression of time has the force of “before” or “until.” tu saprai is future tense, “you will know,” and quanto quell’ arte pesa is an indirect question, literally “how much that art weighs,” meaning how burdensome it is.
segments.inferno-10-g99: E links this request to the preceding speech. se introduces a conditional clause, “if,” while regge is an archaic/Dantean form commonly understood here as “return” or “come again,” with tu as its subject; nel dolce mondo means “to/in the sweet world,” that is, earthly life.
segments.inferno-10-g100: dimmi is an imperative with an attached object pronoun: di’ + mi, “tell me.” It is the main command following the preceding conditional clause.
segments.inferno-10-g101: perché introduces the direct question, “why.” quel popolo è keeps popolo as a singular collective subject, while sì empio means “so cruel/wicked”; incontr’ a’ miei is a contracted form of incontro a i miei, “against/toward my people or kin,” and in ciascuna sua legge means “in each of its laws.”
segments.inferno-10-g102: This target contains only punctuation and adds no independent grammatical content.

sentences.inferno-10-s1: Now my master goes along a hidden path, between the wall of the city and the torments, and I behind his back.
sentences.inferno-10-s2: “O supreme power, who turn me through the wicked circles,” I began, “as it pleases you, speak to me, and satisfy my desires.”
sentences.inferno-10-s3: Could the people who lie among the tombs be seen?
sentences.inferno-10-s4: All the lids are already lifted, and no one keeps guard.”
sentences.inferno-10-s9: “O Tuscan, who go alive through the city of fire, speaking so honorably, may it please you to remain in this place.
sentences.inferno-10-s10: Your speech makes you clearly known as a native of that noble fatherland, to which perhaps I was too troublesome.”
sentences.inferno-10-s11: Suddenly this sound issued from one of the tombs; therefore I, afraid, drew a little closer to my guide.
sentences.inferno-10-s12: And he said to me: “Turn around!”
sentences.inferno-10-s30: Does the sweet light not strike his eyes?
sentences.inferno-10-s32: When he became aware of a certain delay that I was making before answering, he fell back supine and no longer appeared outside.
sentences.inferno-10-s21: Then, at the uncovered opening, a shade rose up alongside this one, as far as its chin; I think it had risen to its knees.
sentences.inferno-10-s22: He looked around me, as if he had a desire to see whether someone else was with me; and when his suspicion was wholly extinguished, weeping he said: “If through this dark prison you go by loftiness of intellect, where is my son?
sentences.inferno-10-s23: and why is he not with you?”
sentences.inferno-10-s5: And he said to me: “They will all be shut when they return here from Jehoshaphat with the bodies they left up there.”
sentences.inferno-10-s6: On this side, Epicurus and all his followers have their cemetery, those who make the soul die with the body.
sentences.inferno-10-s7: Therefore, the question you put to me will soon be answered here within, and so too the desire that you do not mention to me.”
sentences.inferno-10-s8: And I said: “Good guide, I do not keep my heart hidden from you except so as to speak little, and you have not only now prepared me for this.”
sentences.inferno-10-s14: Behold Farinata there, who has risen upright: from the waist up you will see all of him.
sentences.inferno-10-s15: I had already fixed my gaze on his; and he rose up with his chest and forehead as though he held Hell in great scorn.
sentences.inferno-10-s16: And my guide’s brave, ready hands pushed me among the tombs toward him, saying, “Let your words be well considered.”
sentences.inferno-10-s41: It seems that you see, if I hear rightly, beforehand what time brings with it, and that in the present you have another manner of knowing.
sentences.inferno-10-s42: “We see, like one who has poor sight,” he said, “the things that are distant from us; to that extent the supreme Leader still shines upon us.
sentences.inferno-10-s43: When they draw near or are present, our intellect is wholly useless; and if someone else does not bring us news, we know nothing of your human state.
sentences.inferno-10-s44: Therefore you can understand that our knowledge will be wholly dead from that moment when the gate of the future shall be closed.
sentences.inferno-10-s17: When I was at the foot of his tomb, he looked at me for a little while, and then, as if disdainful, asked me: “Who were your ancestors?”
sentences.inferno-10-s19: I, who was eager to obey, did not conceal it from him, but revealed it all to him; whereupon he raised his brows a little upward; then he said: “They were fiercely hostile to me and to my forebears and to my party, so that twice I scattered them.”
sentences.inferno-10-s20: “If they were driven out, they returned from every quarter,” I replied to him, “both the first and the second time; but your people did not learn that art well.”
sentences.inferno-10-s53: Then he moved his foot to the left: we left the wall and went toward the middle along a path that ran into a valley, whose stench was offensive even up there.
sentences.inferno-10-s25: And I to him: “I do not come of my own accord: the one who is waiting over there leads me through this place—perhaps the one whom your Guido held in disdain.”
sentences.inferno-10-s26: His words and the manner of the punishment had already disclosed to me this man’s name; therefore the answer was so full.
sentences.inferno-10-s27: All at once, straightening up, he cried: “How?”
sentences.inferno-10-s49: He set himself in motion; and then, as we went along, he said to me, “Why are you so bewildered?”
sentences.inferno-10-s51: And I satisfied him as to his question.
sentences.inferno-10-s52: “Let your mind preserve what you have heard against yourself,” that sage commanded me; “and now pay attention here,” and he raised his finger: “when you are before the sweet radiance of her whose beautiful eye sees everything, from her you will learn the journey of your life.”
sentences.inferno-10-s45: Then, as if stung with remorse for my fault, I said: “Now then you will tell that fallen one that his son is still joined with the living; and if, just now, I was silent in answer, let him know that I did it because I was already thinking within the error from which you have freed me.”
sentences.inferno-10-s46: And already my master was calling me back; wherefore I begged the spirit all the more quickly to tell me who was with him.
sentences.inferno-10-s47: He said to me: “Here I lie with more than a thousand: in here are Frederick the Second and the Cardinal; and of the others I shall say nothing.”
sentences.inferno-10-s48: Then he hid himself; and I turned my steps toward the ancient poet, thinking back on that speech which seemed hostile to me.
sentences.inferno-10-s37: Then I replied to him: “The carnage and the great slaughter that made the Arbia run red cause such prayers to be made in our temple.”
sentences.inferno-10-s38: After he had moved his head with a sigh, “I was not alone in that,” he said, “nor certainly would I have acted with the others without reason.”
sentences.inferno-10-s39: But I alone was the one who, there where everyone had agreed to do away with Florence, defended her openly.
sentences.inferno-10-s40: “Ah, may your descendants ever find rest,” I begged him, “untie for me that knot which has here entangled my judgment.”
sentences.inferno-10-s33: But that other noble-hearted one, at whose question I had remained, did not change expression, nor move his neck, nor bend his side; and, continuing from his first words, said: “If they have learned that art badly, that torments me more than this bed.”
sentences.inferno-10-s34: But the face of the lady who rules here will not be rekindled fifty times before you will know how heavy that art is.
sentences.inferno-10-s35: And if you ever return to the sweet world, tell me: why is that people so cruel toward my kin in every one of its laws?
